summary:
Millet cultivation in northern China represents one of the earliest independent centers of plant domestication in the world, with the process of bringing foxtail millet (Setaria italica) and broomcorn millet (Panicum miliaceum) under cultivation beginning in the Yellow River basin and northern China plains region around 8000–7000 BCE, broadly contemporaneous with cereal domestication in the Near East. Sites such as [[event:1272|Nanzhuangtou]] in Hebei Province provide early evidence of millet use, with more clearly cultivated material appearing in later sites of the Cishan and Peiligang cultures.

Millet's cultivation was distinct from the rice-based agricultural tradition developing simultaneously in the [[event:6|Yangtze basin]] to the south, representing a separate northern Chinese agricultural system adapted to the drier, colder conditions of the Yellow River region. Foxtail and broomcorn millet are drought-resistant, fast-growing crops well suited to the semi-arid temperate conditions of northern China, and their domestication provided the caloric foundation for the Yellow River valley cultures that would eventually give rise to the Bronze Age Shang civilization.

Millet agriculture in China formed the subsistence base for a long succession of Neolithic cultures including Cishan, Peiligang, Yangshao, and Longshan, each building in complexity and scale on the agricultural foundation established with the earliest millet cultivation. Millet also spread westward along the steppe belt of Eurasia, reaching South Asia and eventually sub-Saharan Africa over subsequent millennia.
